10: A direct revelation: the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost. Luke's mission-statement frames the Zacchaeus encounter as the paradigmatic instance of the Son of Man's seeking-saving mission. The three parables of Luke 15 and the Zacchaeus narrative are the lived theology of Luke 19:10: the lost sheep sought by the shepherd, the lost coin sought by the woman, the lost son welcomed by the father, and now the lost Zacchaeus sought-and-found by the Son of Man himself. The mission of Luke's Gospel is the living-out of this one sentence, fulfilled supremely in the cross (where the Son of Man gives himself to save the utterly lost).
1: Jesus entering Jericho (eiserchomai — he was passing through) positions him at the last stop before Jerusalem. Jericho was a wealthy priestly city; it is also the city Joshua conquered first when Israel entered the Promised Land (Josh 6). The second Joshua (whose Hebrew name Jesus shares) passes through Jericho on the way to the city of David to accomplish a greater conquest: not with trumpets and falling walls but with a cross and an empty tomb. Jericho frames the Zacchaeus encounter geographically as a 'first conquest' moment for the new Joshua's entry into the land.
2: 'Zacchaeus' (from Hebrew zakkay — 'pure/innocent') is a name whose meaning is ironically contradicted by his vocation: the pure man is the chief tax collector (architelōnēs — the only use of this title in the NT), the man others would call most impure. The irony is Christological: the one whose name means 'pure' is found by the one who is himself pure and who makes the impure pure. Chief tax collectors supervised networks of collectors who bought the right to collect taxes and profited by overcharging — Zacchaeus was wealthy through the systemic exploitation his position enabled.
3: 'He was seeking to see who Jesus was' — Zacchaeus's seeking (ezētei idein — he was continually seeking to see) anticipates the shepherd-seeking language of Luke 15 and the Son of Man's mission statement in Luke 19:10. The seeker and the Sought are converging. 'But on account of the crowd he could not, because he was small in stature' — the social dimension: the crowd around Jesus (the righteous, the acceptable) blocked access to Jesus for the man whom they excluded. Christ's mission requires breaking through the crowd's exclusion to reach the one the crowd overlooks.
4: 'So he ran on ahead and climbed up into a sycamore-fig tree to see him, for he was about to pass that way' — Zacchaeus's running and climbing (anabainō — used of going up to Jerusalem, going up to the temple) is the extravagant, dignity-abandoning effort of a wealthy man seeking to see Christ. The sycamore-fig (sykomōraian) was considered a lowly tree whose fruit was food for the poor — the wealthy Zacchaeus climbs a poor man's tree to see the one who has come for the poor. The posture of seeking Christ (running, climbing above the crowd) is itself an image of faith: any dignity must be abandoned to see him.
5: 'When Jesus came to the place, he looked up and said to him, Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for I must stay at your house today' — the divine initiative: Christ looks up (anablepsas) to where Zacchaeus is — the gaze of the Good Shepherd who has come seeking this particular lost sheep. He calls him by name (as the shepherd calls his own sheep by name, John 10:3). 'I must stay' (dei — divine necessity, the same word as Luke 2:49, Luke 4:43, Luke 9:22, Luke 13:33) — the Christ-visit to Zacchaeus's house is a divine appointment, not an accident of the road. Salvation came looking for Zacchaeus before Zacchaeus found it.
6: 'So he hurried and came down and received him joyfully' — Zacchaeus's immediate response (speuō — hurry; katebē — came down at once; hypedesato — welcomed/received) is the model of faith's response to Christ's summons. He does not negotiate, delay, or calculate the social cost of hosting this controversial figure. The joy (chairōn — rejoicing) of his reception echoes the joy of the kingdom's finding (Luke 15:5, 9, 32 — the joy of the shepherd, the woman, the father). The found man rejoices; the rejoicing is not manufactured piety but the natural response to being seen and sought by name.
7: 'And when they saw it, they all grumbled, saying, He has gone in to be the guest of a man who is a sinner' — the crowd's grumbling (diagongyzō — the murmuring of Luke 15:2, the wilderness-murmuring of the LXX) at Jesus's choice of table fellowship is the persistent pattern of religious rejection. They see the act (went to a sinner's house) without seeing the mission (came to seek and save the lost, Luke 19:10). Their grumbling contrasts with Zacchaeus's joy: those outside Christ's purpose grumble at his inclusions; those included by grace rejoice at his arrival.
8: 'And Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord, Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor. And if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore it fourfold' — the changed Zacchaeus stands (statheis — a posture of declaration, public commitment) and speaks kingdom-economics: half of wealth redistributed to the poor (cf. the rich young ruler, Luke 18:22, who would not part with anything), and fourfold restitution for fraud (beyond the Mosaic 20% surcharge of Numbers 5:7 — he offers Zacchaeus-law: 400%). This is not the condition of his salvation but its fruit — the evidence that 'salvation has come to this house' (Luke 19:9) is economic transformation.
9: 'And Jesus said to him, Today salvation has come to this house, since he also is a son of Abraham' — 'today' (sēmeron — the eschatological today of Luke 2:11, Luke 4:21, Luke 23:43) marks the arrival of the kingdom's reality in this home. 'Salvation has come' (sōtēria — the word used for the messianic salvation of Luke 1:69, Luke 1:71, Luke 1:77, Luke 3:6) to this house because the Savior is in this house. 'Son of Abraham' reclaims Zacchaeus for the covenant community from which his occupation excluded him: the tax collector who forfeited his covenant standing by Roman service is restored as a covenant son by Christ's declaration. The covenant comes to him; he does not first come to the covenant.
11: 'As they heard these things, he proceeded to tell a parable, because he was near to Jerusalem, and because they supposed that the kingdom of God was to appear immediately' — the Zacchaeus episode generates false expectations: the crowd, hearing about the kingdom's arrival in this house, expects immediate political-messianic fulfillment at Jerusalem. The parable of the minas corrects this expectation: the kingdom has a delay structure — the king goes away, entrusts assets to servants, and returns for the accounting. The ascension (the king going away) and the parousia (the king's return) frame the present age as the time of faithful stewardship, not immediate triumph.
12: 'He said therefore, A nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom and then return' — the parable's nobleman who travels to receive a kingdom and returns to rule reflects the Herodian practice: Archelaus, son of Herod the Great, literally went to Rome to receive his kingdom from Augustus and returned to rule Judaea. The crowd would recognize the social mechanism. Christ is the greater nobleman who goes away (the Ascension) to receive the kingdom from the Father (Daniel 7:13-14 — 'he was given dominion, glory and a kingdom') and who will return to rule (Revelation 11:15 — 'the kingdoms of the world have become the kingdom of our Lord').
13: 'Calling ten of his servants, he gave them ten minas, and said to them, Engage in business until I come' — the entrusted minas (one mina = approximately three months' wages — a modest but real amount) are the resources of the kingdom entrusted to servants during the master's absence. 'Engage in business until I come' (pragmateusasthe hōs erchomai) is the charter for the church age: the time between Ascension and Parousia is the time for kingdom-faithfulness with entrusted resources. The servant's mandate during the absence is not waiting but working; not preservation but multiplication.
14: 'But his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him, saying, We do not want this man to reign over us' — the citizens' rejection of the nobleman mirrors the Jewish leadership's rejection of Christ's messianic claim. 'We do not want this man to reign over us' is the cry of Luke 23:21 ('Crucify him') and Luke 23:18 ('Away with this man') — the explicit rejection of Christ's lordship by those who should have been his citizens. The parable embeds the Passion narrative within itself: the king who goes away is also the king who is rejected by his own people (John 1:11 — 'he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him').
15: 'When he returned, having received the kingdom, he ordered these servants to whom he had given the money to be called to him, that he might know what they had gained by doing business' — the return of the king with the received kingdom is the Parousia: Christ returns from the Father's presence with the kingdom formally conferred (1 Corinthians 15:24-25 — 'then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power'). The accounting (logos — reckoning) at the return is the eschatological judgment of servants according to their stewardship. The return is certain; only its timing is unspecified.
16: 'The first came before him, saying, Lord, your mina has made ten minas more' — the first servant's tenfold return on the entrusted mina is the maximum fidelity: the one mina given has produced ten through active kingdom-engagement during the master's absence. 'Your mina' acknowledges that the resource was never the servant's own — it was always the master's, entrusted for multiplication. The faithful servant does not confuse stewardship with ownership. Christ who is 'faithful over God's house as a son' (Hebrews 3:6) is the model: he received the Spirit without measure (John 3:34) and multiplied its fruit in his ministry.
17: 'And he said to him, Well done, good servant! Because you have been faithful in a very little, you shall have authority over ten cities' — the commendation 'well done' (euge — a term of warm approval, not merely formal recognition) and the reward-proportioned-to-faithfulness (ten cities for ten minas returned) is the kingdom's economics: present faithful stewardship determines future kingdom-authority. The 'very little' (elachistos) of one mina's stewardship is the training-ground for the enormous authority of the new-creation governance. Those faithful in what seems little to the world are entrusted with what is great in the kingdom.
18: 'And the second came, saying, Lord, your mina has made five minas' — the second servant's fivefold return (compared to the first's tenfold) represents faithful stewardship at a different scale of fruitfulness. Both are commended; the reward is proportional. This is the kingdom's equity: 'to each according to his work' (Revelation 22:12) — not equality of outcome but proportionality of reward to faithfulness. The servant who produces five is not reproached for not producing ten; the servant who produces zero is reproached for not engaging at all (Luke 19:20-24). What matters is faithful engagement, not a predetermined result.
19: 'And he said to him, And you are to be over five cities' — the second servant receives authority over five cities — half of the first servant's reward but still an enormous kingdom-inheritance. The city-authority given to faithful servants in the parable anticipates the new-creation governance language of Revelation (Luke 2:26-27 — 'the one who conquers and who keeps my works until the end... I will give him authority over the nations'; Luke 20:4-6 — reign of the saints). The faithful stewardship of the present age qualifies for participation in the governance of the age to come.
20: 'Then another came, saying, Lord, here is your mina, which I kept laid away in a handkerchief' — the unfaithful servant's preservation of the mina (perfectly intact, no loss, no risk) is the image of fearful religion without engagement. The handkerchief (soudarion — a cloth for wiping sweat) is a mundane, impermanent container for something of kingdom significance. The servant has preserved what he was given without allowing it to do anything. This is the image of the church that buries the gospel in institutional preservation rather than kingdom-extension — keeping it safe instead of multiplying it.
21: 'For I was afraid of you, because you are a severe man. You take what you did not deposit, and reap what you did not sow' — the unfaithful servant's fear-logic (the master is hard, so I played it safe) reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of the master's character. He knows the master's severity but does not know the master's generosity (the entrusting of resources, the promise of reward). The servant who fears the master's demands without trusting the master's generosity will be paralyzed. This is the difference between legalistic religion (fear of God's demands) and gospel-shaped faithfulness (trust in God's generosity enabling obedient engagement).
22: 'He said to him, I will condemn you with your own words, you wicked servant!' — the servant is judged by his own stated reasoning: if you knew I was a severe man who takes what I didn't deposit, then precisely for that reason you should have put the mina in the bank rather than in a handkerchief. The logic of his own fear-assessment condemns him: his inaction is inconsistent even with his stated understanding of the master. The eschatological judgment uses the servant's own reasoning as the standard — 'by your words you will be justified, and by your words you will be condemned' (Matthew 12:37).
23: 'Why then did you not put my money in the bank, and at my return I might have collected it with interest?' — the minimum requirement (bank interest) is the floor that even the fearful servant could have met. The servant did not meet even the minimum. The parable teaches that there is no neutral position in kingdom-stewardship: inaction is itself a choice with consequences. Doing nothing with what Christ has entrusted is not a safe option — it is a guaranteed loss. The cross was not Christ's 'safe option' (Gethsemane, Luke 22:42); his faithful engagement with the Father's mission, at maximum cost, is the model of what kingdom-stewardship looks like.
24: 'And he said to those who stood by, Take the mina from him, and give it to the one who has the ten minas' — the reallocation of the unfaithful servant's mina to the one with ten is the kingdom's economics in its most confronting form: to the faithful, more is entrusted; from the unfaithful, what little they have is taken. This is the operating principle of spiritual growth: faithfulness in the received creates capacity for more; unfaithfulness atrophies the capacity received. The faithful servant's ten-city authority is the result of ten-mina faithfulness; the unfaithful servant's loss is the result of zero-mina faithfulness.
25: 'And they said to him, Lord, he has ten minas!' — the bystanders' objection to giving the already-wealthy servant more captures the common-sense fairness objection to the kingdom's economics. Why give the already-productive servant more? The kingdom's economics are not distributive justice (equal shares to all) but productive justice (resources to those who will use them). Christ who has received all authority (Matthew 28:18 — 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me') and to whom all things are being subjected (1 Corinthians 15:27-28) is the supreme recipient of the kingdom's 'to the faithful, more.' He receives everything because he was faithful in everything.
26: 'I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given, but from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away' — the kingdom's axiom of spiritual multiplication is the law of grace: grace given and received produces more grace; grace given and unused — treated as a possession to hoard rather than a resource to deploy — is lost. This is the Reformation insight about faith: saving faith is always living faith (active, engaging, producing fruit); dead faith is not merely insufficient — it demonstrates that the faith was never truly present. What appears to be preserved faith that produces nothing may be no faith at all.
27: 'But as for these enemies of mine, who did not want me to reign over them, bring them here and slaughter them before me' — the judgment of the citizens who refused the king's reign is the eschatological judgment language of the parable's political frame. This is not a prescriptive model for Christian violence but the parable's portrayal of the Parousia's judgment: those who have persistently refused Christ's lordship throughout the age of stewardship will face his judgment at his return. Revelation 19:11-21 uses similar language for the same reality: the returning king's judgment of those who opposed him. The parable ends with both the reward of faithfulness and the judgment of refusal.
28: 'And when he had said these things, he went on ahead, going up to Jerusalem' — the resumed journey to Jerusalem (anabainōn — going up, the standard verb for ascending to Jerusalem) marks the resumption of the Passion journey after the Jericho parenthesis (Zacchaeus, minas parable). 'Going up ahead' (emprosthen poreuomenos — going before them) is the image of the shepherd going before the flock (John 10:4 — 'he goes before them, and the sheep follow him'). Christ leads the way to the city where he will die. His going up is voluntary, purposeful, and ahead of all who follow — he arrives at the cross before any disciple arrives at their own.
29: 'When he drew near to Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount that is called Olivet, he sent two of the disciples' — the Mount of Olives is the prophetic location of messianic arrival (Zechariah 14:4 — 'On that day his feet shall stand on the Mount of Olives that lies before Jerusalem on the east') and of Christ's return (Acts 1:11-12 — the disciples return from Olivet after the Ascension, told that 'this Jesus... will come in the same way'). The triumphal entry from the Mount of Olives is the first-coming fulfillment of Zechariah's messianic arrival; the Ascension from the same mount points to the second-coming fulfillment.
30: 'Saying, Go into the village in front of you, where on entering you will find a colt tied, on which no one has ever yet sat. Untie it and bring it here' — the unridden colt is a detail of ritual purity: a new animal not broken for human use is appropriate for sacred purposes (Numbers 19:2 — the red heifer 'which has never been yoked'; 1 Samuel 6:7 — the new cart with cows that 'have never borne a yoke'). Christ comes to Jerusalem on a vehicle consecrated by its newness to his use. The colt that has never carried a human burden will carry the one who will bear every human burden. The arranged colt also demonstrates Christ's foreknowledge and sovereignty over events.
31: 'If anyone asks you, Why are you untying it? you shall say this: The Lord has need of it' — 'the Lord has need' (ho Kyrios autou chreian echei — the Lord needs it) is the claim of divine sovereignty in ordinary provision. The title 'Lord' (Kyrios) applied to Jesus here is the same as the LXX's title for YHWH. The Lord of the universe who needs nothing (Acts 17:25 — 'he is not served by human hands, as though he needed anything') paradoxically 'needs' a colt — the same paradox as the Word who needs nothing assuming flesh, the King who needs nothing needing a manger. His 'need' is always the need of the mission, not the need of lack.
32: 'So those who were sent went away and found it just as he had told them' — the disciples' finding of the colt exactly as Christ described confirms his prophetic foreknowledge. This small fulfillment (colt exactly where he said) prepares the disciples to trust his larger prophetic word about Jerusalem's destruction (Luke 19:41-44), his death (Luke 9:22, Luke 18:31-33), and his resurrection. Christ's precision in small details is the ground of faith for his large promises. Every verified small prophecy strengthens confidence in the unverified large ones.
33: 'And as they were untying the colt, its owners said to them, Why are you untying the colt?' — the owners' question (the same question Christ had anticipated in Luke 19:31) is answered by the disciples' use of the prescribed phrase. The prepared answer serves as the key that unlocks the colt's release. This is the pattern of kingdom provision through prophetic preparation: Christ announces; disciples obey; provision follows. The owners' willingness to release their colt to 'the Lord's need' is itself a small act of faith — they yield to a claim they have not directly heard from its source.
34: 'And they said, The Lord has need of it' — the disciples' repetition of Christ's phrase (ho Kyrios autou chreian echei) as the key that releases the colt is the model of proclamation: repeating what Christ has said, not generating new authority from themselves. The disciples' authority to release the colt comes from the word they carry, not from their own status. This is the apostolic pattern: 'we do not preach ourselves, but Jesus Christ as Lord, with ourselves as your servants for Jesus's sake' (2 Corinthians 4:5). The phrase 'the Lord has need' opens every door that needs to be opened for the mission.
35: 'And they brought it to Jesus, and throwing their cloaks on the colt, they set Jesus on it' — the disciples' cloaks thrown on the colt are the improvised saddle of a spontaneous but prophetically planned event. The act echoes 2 Kings 9:13 — when Jehu was acclaimed king, 'they hurried and took their garments and put them under him on the bare steps.' The disciples' garment-laying is the gesture of royal acclamation: they are making a throne for the king. Their recognition (however imperfect) of Christ's royalty is the beginning of the Palm Sunday proclamation that will reach its crescendo at Luke 19:38.
36: 'And as he rode along, they spread their cloaks on the road' — the spreading of cloaks on the road is the ancient gesture of honor-pavment before royalty (2 Kings 9:13 again — the surface made worthy of the king's passage). The road of Christ's entry becomes a carpet of the disciples' possessions. The irony is that the king who comes in meekness on an unridden colt (the warrior-king rides a warhorse; Zechariah 9:9 — 'he is humble and mounted on a donkey') receives a warrior-king's honor. He comes in peace; they honor him as a victor. The tension between the mode of his coming and the mode of his reception captures the Passion week's paradox.
37: 'As he was drawing near — already on the way down the Mount of Olives — the whole multitude of his disciples began to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice for all the mighty works they had seen' — the praise-burst (chairontes — rejoicing) on the Mount of Olives descent recalls the joy of angels at the birth (Luke 2:10-14 — 'great joy... glory to God in the highest') and anticipates the joy of the Resurrection (Luke 24:52 — 'they returned to Jerusalem with great joy'). The mighty works (dynameis — the healings, exorcisms, resurrections) are the grounds of the praise: they have seen who he is by what he has done.
38: 'Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!' — the disciples' acclamation quotes Psalms 118:26 ('Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD!') with the addition of 'the King' — making explicit what the Psalm implies. 'Peace in heaven and glory in the highest' inverts the angels' birth-announcement (Luke 2:14 — 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace') — from earth-directed peace to heaven-directed peace. The kingdom's arrival in the King's person is the making-of-peace between heaven and earth that his cross will accomplish (Colossians 1:20 — 'through him to reconcile all things... making peace by the blood of his cross').
39: 'And some of the Pharisees in the crowd said to him, Teacher, rebuke your disciples' — the Pharisees' demand that Jesus silence the disciples is the demand to suppress the messianic acclamation. Their address ('Teacher' — Didaskale, not 'Lord') positions them outside the acclamation. They hear the disciples' praise as blasphemous presumption or dangerous sedition; Jesus hears it as the right response to who he is. The Pharisees who cannot receive the disciples' praise of the King will hear from the stones instead (Luke 19:40). Those who suppress the creation's praise do not silence it — they remove themselves from it.
40: 'He answered, I tell you, if these were silent, the stones would cry out' — the stones crying out (hoi lithoi kraxousin) is the declaration that the creation itself recognizes its King's arrival. The stones of creation would praise what the Pharisees would suppress. This is the Christological claim embedded in creation-language: the King who comes is the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16), and the creation — though it groans (Romans 8:22) — recognizes its Maker. Every created thing is a potential witness to Christ's glory; the Pharisees who suppress human praise cannot suppress the creation's.
41: 'And when he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it' — Jesus weeping over Jerusalem (eklaien — he wept, the same verb as John 11:35 at Lazarus's tomb) in the middle of the triumphal procession is the supreme irony of Palm Sunday: the King acclaimed by the crowd weeps over the city that will reject him. His tears are not self-pity but prophetic grief: he sees the coming destruction that Jerusalem's rejection of him will bring. The weeping King is the image of the God who weeps over Israel's judgment (Jeremiah 8:21 — 'I am hurt at the grief of my people'; Isaiah 22:4 — 'I will weep bitterly'). God has come in person to weep over his city.
42: 'Saying, Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes' — 'the things that make for peace' (ta pros eirēnēn) are the terms of the kingdom that Christ offers: repentance, recognition of the visitation, reception of the King. Jerusalem has not recognized what makes for peace because her spiritual eyes are closed — not by divine arbitrariness but by the accumulated rejection that has hardened the city's heart. 'Hidden from your eyes' is the divine passive of judicial blindness: the long refusal of the prophets (Luke 13:34) has brought the concealment of salvific knowledge. Christ weeps at the blindness his judgment makes necessary.
43: 'For the days will come upon you, when your enemies will set up a barricade around you and surround you and hem you in on every side' — the prophecy of Jerusalem's siege is the historical prediction fulfilled in 70 CE when Titus and the Roman army surrounded Jerusalem with a wall of circumvallation (Josephus, Jewish War V.12.1-2 describes the Roman siege works in detail). Christ's prophetic specificity (the barricade, the surrounding, the hemming in) is the precision of divine foreknowledge. The coming judgment is the natural consequence of rejecting the one who offered peace (Luke 19:42); it is not divine vengeance but the inevitable result of refusing the peace-maker.
44: 'And tear you down to the ground, you and your children within you. And they will not leave one stone upon another in you, because you did not know the time of your visitation' — 'the time of your visitation' (ton kairon tēs episkopēs sou — the season of God's coming-to-visit) is the Incarnation-moment: God has come in person to Jerusalem, walking its streets, teaching in its temple, weeping at its walls. Jerusalem did not 'know' this visitation — she did not recognize her God in the flesh. The consequence (stones torn down) is proportionate to the missed opportunity: the city that missed the living Stone (1 Peter 2:4-8) will have no stones left standing.
45: 'And he entered the temple and began to drive out those who sold' — the temple cleansing follows immediately upon the entry and the weeping-over-Jerusalem. The cleansing of the temple is a prophetic-symbolic act: the King who arrives to his city (Luke 19:38) and finds corruption in his Father's house (Luke 2:49 — 'my Father's house') acts with prophetic authority to restore its purpose. The short Lukan account (without the money-changers and dove-sellers detail of Matthew/Mark) focuses the act: driving out those who have made the temple a commercial space. Christ's action is the enacted judgment of his prophetic words about the temple (Luke 19:44 — the stones torn down).
46: 'Saying to them, It is written, My house shall be a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers' — the quotation combines Isaiah 56:7 ('my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples') and Jeremiah 7:11 ('Has this house, which is called by my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes?'). The Jeremiah context (Jer 7) is the prophet's temple sermon warning that the temple will be destroyed like Shiloh if Israel's wickedness continues — a sermon that led to Jeremiah's near-death (Jer 26). Christ's use of Jeremiah in the temple carries the same weight: judgment is coming on the house that has become a den of robbers, as it came on Shiloh.
47: 'And he was teaching daily in the temple. The chief priests and the scribes and the principal men of the people were seeking to destroy him' — the daily teaching in the temple (Luke 21:37 confirms this pattern) is Christ's occupation of his Father's house during the Passion week. He comes each day to the place where the authorities seek his death and teaches without protection other than the crowd (Luke 19:48). This is the posture of the King who knows he is going to the cross and teaches freely until the hour arrives. His boldness is not bravado but confidence in the Father's timing: 'my hour has not yet come' (John 2:4; Luke 7:6, 8; Luke 8:20; Luke 13:1).
48: 'But they did not find anything they could do, for all the people were hanging on his words' — the people's attentiveness (exekremato autou — were hanging on him, the verb used for hanging on a cliff-edge) is the literal protection of Christ's life during Passion week: the authorities could not act because the crowds would riot (Luke 22:2 — 'they feared the people'). Christ's popularity is the human shield that preserves him until the hour the Father has determined. The Father's protection is accomplished through ordinary means: a crowd of people who cannot get enough of what Jesus is saying. Providence works through the attentiveness of those who hang on his words.
